skill.name: agentic-workflow
skill.description: Guides AI agent productivity workflows for Claude, Codex, Claude Code, Gemini, Git, MCP servers, sessions, and container-based experimentation.
skill.summary: Use structured AI agent workflows for coding, review, testing, Git, MCP, and session management.
skill.author: supercent-io

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported many command, filesystem, network, and environment-access patterns. Most are false positives from Markdown command tables and example prompts, but the Docker example includes a remote install script piped to a shell and passes an API key into a container. No prompt injection attempt or confirmed malicious intent was found, so this should publish only with a warning about command review.

medium_findings.title: Remote Install Script Piped To Shell
medium_findings.description: The Dockerfile example downloads an installer from a URL and pipes it directly to a shell. This is a real copy-and-run supply-chain risk because users may execute unreviewed remote code during container setup.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The risky command pattern is present in an executable Dockerfile block. The context is instructional rather than malicious, so the confidence is high for risk but not for malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: API Key Forwarded Into Container
medium_findings.description: The Docker run example forwards ANTHROPIC_API_KEY from the host into a container. This can expose credentials to container processes and should be used only with trusted images and minimal privileges.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment variable name clearly identifies a secret-bearing API key. The example is legitimate for tool use, but it increases credential exposure if copied without review.

low_findings.title: Command Examples Require User Review
low_findings.description: The skill contains many command examples for Git, Docker, Codex, Claude, Gemini, and MCP workflows. These are mostly documentation examples, but users should review commands before execution because the skill allows Bash-oriented workflows.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible in Markdown examples and are relevant to the workflow skill. Most are not suspicious by intent, but they can affect local projects when followed.

low_findings.title: Filesystem Path Examples Are Benign Workflow Guidance
low_findings.description: The hidden shell profile and parent-directory worktree examples are legitimate development workflow instructions. They are not evidence of stealthy file access or path traversal in executable code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file paths appear in user-facing setup examples for aliases and Git worktrees. No code reads hidden files or traverses paths automatically.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto And Obfuscation Flags Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The static scanner flagged weak cryptography and suspicious combinations, but the reviewed lines contain metadata, workflow headings, command references, or quick-reference text. No encryption code, obfuscation layer, credential exfiltration, or prompt injection attempt was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed context does not contain cryptographic implementation or hidden behavior. These alerts appear to be keyword or heuristic false positives.

dangerous_patterns.title: Pipe To Shell Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: A remote installer is fetched over HTTPS and executed immediately by the shell. Users should verify the installer source and checksum before running this pattern.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is exact and appears in a Dockerfile command. Its risk is well understood even though the surrounding intent is instructional.

dangerous_patterns.title: Host Secret Passed To Container
dangerous_patterns.description: A host API key is passed into a Docker container through an environment variable. This can leak secrets if the container image or runtime is not trusted.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The line directly forwards ANTHROPIC_API_KEY. Legitimate container workflows may need this, but it is still sensitive credential handling.

content.value_statement: Teams lose time when AI agent sessions, commands, Git tasks, and MCP tools are handled inconsistently. This skill provides practical workflow patterns for Claude, Codex, Claude Code, Gemini, Git, containers, and multi-agent coordination.
actual_capabilities: Summarizes key Claude Code, Codex, and Gemini CLI commands for daily development work.
Provides session management patterns for continuing, resuming, naming, and resetting agent conversations.
Gives Git workflow prompts for commit messages, draft pull requests, worktrees, and PR review.
Explains MCP server use cases and recommends limiting active servers and tools.
Provides multi-agent workflow patterns for planning, analysis, coding, testing, and reporting.
Includes container workflow examples for isolated experimentation with AI agents.
limitations: It is a guidance skill, not an automation script or command runner.
Command examples must be reviewed before use in real projects or production systems.
MCP server availability depends on the user environment and installed integrations.
Some referenced CLI features may change across tool versions.

use_cases.title: Standardize Daily Agent Commands
use_cases.description: Use a concise command and shortcut reference to run common Claude Code, Codex, and Gemini workflows.

use_cases.title: Coordinate Multi-Agent Development
use_cases.description: Split planning, large-context analysis, implementation, testing, and final reporting across specialized AI tools.

use_cases.title: Improve Git Review Flow
use_cases.description: Use structured prompts for worktrees, pull request summaries, security review, improvement passes, and test execution.

prompt_templates.title: Start A Clean Agent Session
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a new coding task
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the agentic workflow skill to help me start a clean session for this repository. Identify the first commands I should run and how I should manage context.

prompt_templates.title: Create A Git Workflow Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the agentic workflow skill to design a Git workflow for this change. Include branch or worktree setup, review steps, tests, and commit guidance.

prompt_templates.title: Coordinate Multi-Agent Implementation
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the agentic workflow skill to split this feature across planning, codebase analysis, implementation, testing, and final reporting. Assign each step to the best available agent.

prompt_templates.title: Audit And Optimize Agent Operations
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the agentic workflow skill to audit my current AI agent setup. Review commands, MCP servers, context usage, Git flow, and session recovery practices, then recommend improvements.

output_examples.input: Plan a safe workflow for reviewing a pull request with AI assistance.
output: Checkout the pull request in an isolated branch or worktree.
Ask the agent to summarize changed files and intended behavior.
Request a focused review for security, performance, and missing tests.
Apply accepted fixes, run the project test suite, and prepare a concise review summary.

output_examples.input: Set up a multi-agent workflow for a new API feature.
output: Use Claude or Claude Code to define the API shape and acceptance criteria.
Use Gemini for large-context analysis of existing API patterns.
Use Claude or Codex to implement the selected design.
Use Codex to run tests and builds, then synthesize the final result.

output_examples.input: Recover when an AI coding session becomes overloaded.
output: Check context usage and tool count.
Create a short handoff note with decisions, files, and pending work.
Start a fresh session or resume a named session.
Load only the files needed for the next task.

best_practices: Review every generated shell command before running it, especially commands that install software or use secrets.
Keep MCP servers and active tools limited to the services needed for the current task.
Use worktrees or containers for risky experiments so main project state remains recoverable.
anti_patterns: Do not run remote install scripts or container commands without checking the source and permissions.
Do not keep long, polluted conversations when a concise handoff and fresh session would be clearer.
Do not give every agent full project, shell, network, and credential access by default.

faq.question: Does this skill execute commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides workflow guidance and command examples. Users or their AI tool decide what to execute.

faq.question: Which tools does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers Claude, Claude Code, Codex, Gemini, Git, MCP servers, Docker, and common session workflows.

faq.question: Is it safe for production repositories?
faq.answer: It can be used with production repositories if commands are reviewed and risky work is isolated in branches, worktrees, or containers.

faq.question: Why is there a security warning?
faq.answer: The skill includes command examples, a remote install pipeline, and API key forwarding into a container. These patterns require user review.

faq.question: Can it help with pull request reviews?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes prompts for checking out PRs, summarizing changes, reviewing focused files, improving code, and running tests.

faq.question: Does it require MCP servers?
faq.answer: No. MCP guidance is optional and depends on which servers are installed in the user environment.
